summary: Joel briefly describes a bug that only affected users running Hebrew or Greek versions of Windows 2000. With the help of beta testers, developer Michael tracked it down to a library using Chr(255) as a separator character, which got remapped through non-Latin code pages. They used VMWare to install Hebrew Windows 2000 for debugging. The post illustrates how character encoding assumptions can cause subtle, locale-specific bugs.
key_points: A library's use of Chr(255) as a separator character caused bugs on non-Latin Windows locales
The bug only manifested on Hebrew and Greek versions of Windows 2000 due to code page character remapping
VMWare was used as a practical tool for reproducing locale-specific bugs without dedicated hardware
Beta testers were essential in identifying and tracking down the internationalization issue
money_quotes: One of the libraries we were using was using Chr(255) as a separator character in lists.
This got mapped through the Hebrew and Greek code pages to a different character (y umlaut was mapped to plain y).
key_insight: Using high-ASCII characters as sentinel values in code is fragile because code page mappings differ across locales, causing subtle bugs for international users.
